Test Scenario / Title: Each row = one test case. Fill columns A–X for every test.
Test Objective / Purpose: Severity: Critical = app crashes or patient data is lost; Major = feature is broken but there is a workaround; Minor = small issue with low impact on daily use; Trivial/Cosmetic = just a typo or color mismatch.
Preconditions: Priority: P0 = must fix before go-live (blocks patient care); P1 = fix soon — high impact; P2 = fix when possible — medium impact; P3 = fix later — low impact.
Test Data / Inputs: Status: Not Tested = not started; Pass = works as expected; Fail = something went wrong; Blocked = cannot test because something else is broken; N/A = does not apply to this environment.
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute: Write steps as numbered instructions like: 1. Open the Login page, 2. Click the Google login button, etc. Write as if explaining to someone who has never used a computer before.
Expected Result: Describe exactly what the user should see if the app works correctly.
Actual Result: Leave blank before testing. Fill in what actually happened after you test.
Status: Use dropdown: Not Tested / Pass / Fail / Blocked / N/A
Bug ID: Only fill if Status = Fail. Use format BUG-001, BUG-002, etc.
Bug Description: Write a simple sentence about what went wrong — avoid technical jargon (e.g. say 'the page went blank' instead of 'null reference error').
Steps to Reproduce Bug: Copy from column G if same, or write adjusted steps if needed.
Actual Result (Bug): Same as Actual Result (column I) when test fails.
Expected Result (Bug): Same as Expected Result (column H) when test fails.
Environment / Operating System: Example: Windows 11, macOS Ventura, Android 14, iOS 18
Fixed in Build / Release Version: Fill after bug is fixed, e.g. v1.2.3
Attachment / Logs / Screenshot: Attach screenshot, video link, or paste the error message you saw on screen
Comments / Notes: Any extra notes, re-test results, or related requirements

Test Scenario / Title: Filter by Status = Fail to see all bugs quickly. Filter by Module to test one area at a time.
Test Objective / Purpose: Color code rows in Excel: Green = Pass, Red = Fail, Yellow = Blocked, Grey = Not Tested. This makes it easy to see progress at a glance.
Preconditions: User Roles in DentalHub: Super Admin (manages all hospitals), Hospital Admin (manages one hospital), Doctor, Receptionist, Patient. Each role has different access rights.
Test Data / Inputs: Modules: Login (Google OAuth), Landing Page, Hospital Dashboard, Patients, Doctors, Appointments, Visits, Dental Chart, Treatments, Prescriptions (with PDF), Billing/Invoices, Reports, Settings, Admin Panel (hospital approvals/subscriptions), Patient Portal.

Test Scenario / Title: Successful login with Google account
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that a registered user can sign in with their Google account and be redirected to the correct dashboard based on their role.
Preconditions: The DentalHub login page is open in the browser. The user already has a Google account linked to a hospital_admin or doctor profile.
Test Data / Inputs: Google Account: testdoctor@gmail.com (role: hospital_admin)
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Open the DentalHub app in your browser (e.g. https://dentizhub.netlify.app).
2. Click the 'Login' or 'Get Started' button on the landing page.
3. On the Login page, click the 'Continue with Google' button.
4. In the Google popup, select or enter your Google account: testdoctor@gmail.com.
5. Allow any permissions Google asks for.
6. Wait for the page to load after signing in.
Expected Result: The app should redirect you to the Hospital Dashboard (/dashboard) page. You should see stats like Total Patients, Today's Appointments, Today's Visits, and Total Doctors. Your name should appear in the sidebar or header.
Actual Result: Dashboard loaded successfully. Stats cards visible (Total Patients, Today's Appointments, etc.). User name shown in sidebar.
Comments / Notes: Role-based redirect works correctly — hospital_admin goes to /dashboard.

Test Scenario / Title: Login redirects Super Admin to Admin Panel
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that a Super Admin user is automatically redirected to the Admin Dashboard instead of the hospital dashboard.
Preconditions: Login page is open. User's Google account is linked to a super_admin profile.
Test Data / Inputs: Google Account: superadmin@dentalhub.com (role: super_admin)
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Open the DentalHub login page.
2. Click 'Continue with Google'.
3. Sign in with the super admin Google account.
4. Wait for the page to load.
Expected Result: The app should redirect you to the Super Admin Dashboard (/admin) page. You should see stats like Total Hospitals, Pending Approvals, Frozen Hospitals, and Expiring Subscriptions.
Actual Result: Redirected to /admin. Super Admin Dashboard displayed with all stats.
Comments / Notes: Super admin role detection works.

2. Click 'Continue with Google'.
3. Sign in with the patient Google account.
4. Wait for the page to load.

Actual Result: Redirected to /patient. Patient dashboard shows upcoming appointments and medical history section.

Test Scenario / Title: Already logged-in user cannot see login page
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that when a user is already signed in, visiting the login page redirects them to their dashboard automatically.
Preconditions: User is already logged in as hospital_admin.
Test Data / Inputs: No additional input needed. Just try to visit /login.
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Make sure you are already logged in (you can see the Dashboard).
2. In the browser address bar, manually type the login URL (e.g. https://dentizhub.netlify.app/login) and press Enter.
3. Watch what happens.
Expected Result: The app should automatically redirect you back to your dashboard. You should NOT see the login page if you are already signed in.
Actual Result: Redirected back to /dashboard automatically. Login page was not shown.
Comments / Notes: Auth redirect guard works.

Test Scenario / Title: Google login error shows friendly message
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that if Google login fails for any reason, the app shows a clear and helpful error message instead of crashing.
Preconditions: Login page is open. Simulate a login failure (e.g. cancel the Google popup).
Test Data / Inputs: Action: Cancel the Google sign-in popup window.
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Open the Login page.
2. Click 'Continue with Google'.
3. When the Google sign-in popup appears, click the X button to close/cancel it.
4. Look at the Login page for any error message.
Expected Result: The app should show a toast notification or error message like 'Authentication failed. Please try again.' The login page should remain usable — no crash, no blank screen.
Actual Result: No error message appeared after canceling. The page stayed on the login screen but the button was stuck showing a spinner that never stopped.
Bug Description: When a user cancels the Google sign-in popup, the 'Continue with Google' button gets stuck in a loading/spinner state and never recovers. The user has to refresh the entire page to try logging in again.
Steps to Reproduce Bug:
1. Open Login page.
2. Click 'Continue with Google'.
3. Cancel/close the Google popup.
4. Observe the button — it stays in loading state forever.
Actual Result (Bug): Button shows spinning loader indefinitely. No error message. Button becomes unusable without page refresh.
Expected Result (Bug): Button should return to normal state after popup is canceled. An error toast should appear saying 'Sign in was cancelled. Please try again.'
Comments / Notes: The setIsGoogleLoading(false) may not be called when user cancels OAuth popup.

Test Scenario / Title: Add doctor with missing required fields
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the form does not submit when mandatory fields (like name) are left empty.
Preconditions: Doctors page is open with the 'Add Doctor' form visible.
Test Data / Inputs: Name: (empty) | All other fields: empty
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Go to Doctors page.
2. Click 'Add Doctor'.
3. Leave all fields empty — do NOT type anything.
4. Click 'Save'.
Expected Result: The form should show validation messages (e.g. 'Doctor name is required'). The form should NOT submit. No doctor should be added to the list.
Actual Result: The form submitted with empty fields and created a doctor record with a blank name. The doctor appears in the list as an empty card.
Bug Description: The 'Add Doctor' form has no client-side validation. It allows submission with completely empty fields, creating a doctor record with no name, no specialization, and no contact info. This creates garbage data in the system.
Steps to Reproduce Bug:
1. Go to Doctors page.
2. Click 'Add Doctor'.
3. Leave ALL fields empty.
4. Click 'Save'.
5. See the blank doctor card appear in the list.
Actual Result (Bug): Form submitted successfully with empty fields. A blank doctor record was created.
Expected Result (Bug): Form should show validation errors like 'Doctor name is required' and prevent submission until required fields are filled.
Comments / Notes: Needs form validation — at minimum require name, specialization, and phone.

Test Scenario / Title: Schedule a new appointment
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that a receptionist or admin can create a new appointment for a patient with a specific doctor on a specific date and time.
Preconditions: User is logged in. At least 1 patient and 1 doctor exist in the system. Appointments page (/dashboard/appointments) is open.
Test Data / Inputs: Patient: Rajesh Kumar | Doctor: Dr. Anjali Sharma | Date: 2026-03-10 | Time: 10:30 AM | Duration: 30 minutes | Reason: Teeth cleaning
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Go to Appointments page from sidebar.
2. Click 'New Appointment' or '+' button.
3. Select Patient: Rajesh Kumar (from dropdown).
4. Select Doctor: Dr. Anjali Sharma (from dropdown).
5. Set Date: 2026-03-10.
6. Set Time: 10:30 AM.
7. Set Duration: 30 minutes.
8. Enter Reason: Teeth cleaning.
9. Click 'Create Appointment' or 'Save'.
Expected Result: A success toast should appear. The appointment should appear in the appointments list with status 'Scheduled'. It should show the patient name, doctor name, date, and time.
Actual Result: Appointment created. Toast: 'Appointment created!' Entry visible in list with status: Scheduled.

Test Scenario / Title: Schedule overlapping appointment for same doctor
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the system warns or prevents booking two appointments for the same doctor at the same date and time.
Preconditions: Appointments page is open. Dr. Anjali Sharma already has an appointment on 2026-03-10 at 10:30 AM.
Test Data / Inputs: Patient: Meera Patel | Doctor: Dr. Anjali Sharma | Date: 2026-03-10 | Time: 10:30 AM | Duration: 30 minutes | Reason: Root Canal consultation
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Go to Appointments page.
2. Click 'New Appointment'.
3. Select a different patient: Meera Patel.
4. Select the SAME doctor: Dr. Anjali Sharma.
5. Set the SAME date and time: 2026-03-10, 10:30 AM.
6. Click 'Create Appointment'.
Expected Result: The app should show an error: 'Dr. Anjali Sharma already has an appointment at this time. Please choose a different time slot.' The overlapping appointment should NOT be created.
Actual Result: The second appointment was created without any warning. Dr. Anjali Sharma now has TWO patients booked at 10:30 AM on the same day.
Bug Description: The system does not check for double-booking. A doctor can have multiple patients scheduled at the exact same date and time. This will cause confusion in the clinic — the doctor cannot see two patients simultaneously.
Steps to Reproduce Bug:
1. Create an appointment for Dr. Anjali Sharma on 2026-03-10 at 10:30 AM.
2. Create another appointment for the same doctor on the same date and time.
3. Both appointments are saved without any warning.
Actual Result (Bug): Two appointments exist for the same doctor at the same time.
Expected Result (Bug): System should prevent overlapping appointments for the same doctor and show a conflict warning.
Comments / Notes: Critical — this directly impacts patient care and scheduling.

Test Scenario / Title: Move visit from Waiting to In Progress to Completed

6. Verify status changes to 'Completed'.
Expected Result: After clicking Start: status should change to 'In Progress' (with a blue or amber badge). After clicking Complete: status should change to 'Completed' (green badge). Each transition should show a success toast.
Actual Result: Start → status changed to In Progress. Complete → status changed to Completed. Toasts appeared for both.

Test Scenario / Title: Record a tooth condition on the dental chart
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that a doctor can click on a tooth in the interactive dental chart and record its condition.
Preconditions: User is logged in. Dental Chart page (/dashboard/dental-chart) is open. A patient is selected.
Test Data / Inputs: Patient: Rajesh Kumar | Tooth Number: 19 (Lower Left 2nd Molar) | Condition: Cavity | Notes: Deep cavity on occlusal surface, needs filling
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Go to Dental Chart page from sidebar.
2. Select patient: Rajesh Kumar from the patient dropdown.
3. On the interactive tooth diagram, click on tooth number 19 (lower left area).
4. In the popup or side panel that appears, select Condition: Cavity.
5. Enter Notes: Deep cavity on occlusal surface, needs filling.
6. Click 'Save' or 'Record'.
Expected Result: Tooth 19 should change color on the chart to indicate a recorded condition (e.g. red for cavity). The condition details should be saved. A success toast should appear.
Actual Result: Tooth 19 highlighted in red. Condition saved. Toast: 'Condition recorded.' Notes visible when clicking tooth again.
Comments / Notes: Interactive dental chart is intuitive.

Test Scenario / Title: Click a tooth without selecting a patient first
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the dental chart shows a helpful message when no patient is selected.
Preconditions: Dental Chart page is open. No patient is selected in the dropdown.
Test Data / Inputs: No patient selected | Click any tooth
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Go to Dental Chart page.
2. Do NOT select any patient from the patient dropdown.
3. Click on any tooth in the chart (e.g. tooth 1).
Expected Result: The app should show a message: 'Please select a patient first' or disable clicking on teeth until a patient is chosen. It should NOT crash or show a blank error.
Actual Result: App showed an error toast: 'Error saving condition' and the console showed 'patient_id is required'. The tooth still changed color visually even though nothing was saved.
Bug Description: When no patient is selected and a user clicks on a tooth in the dental chart, the app tries to save the condition without a patient ID. This results in an error. Additionally, the tooth visually changes color (suggesting it was saved) even though the save actually failed — misleading the user.
Steps to Reproduce Bug:
1. Go to Dental Chart page.
2. Do NOT select a patient.
3. Click on any tooth.
4. Try to save a condition.
5. See error toast, but tooth color changes anyway.
Actual Result (Bug): Error toast appeared. Tooth color changed visually even though data was not saved.
Expected Result (Bug): Chart should require patient selection first. Teeth should be unclickable or greyed out until a patient is chosen.
Comments / Notes: UX issue — visual state is misleading. Add patient validation before chart interaction.

Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the hospital's primary and secondary branding colors can be changed in settings.
Preconditions: Settings page is open. User is hospital_admin.
Test Data / Inputs: Primary Color: #2563eb (blue) | Secondary Color: #7c3aed (purple)
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Go to Settings page.
2. Find the Branding or Appearance section.
3. Change Primary Color by entering: #2563eb.
4. Change Secondary Color by entering: #7c3aed.
5. Click 'Save'.
6. Navigate to the hospital's public page (if available) to see if colors changed.
Expected Result: Colors should be saved successfully. If the hospital has a public-facing page, the new colors should be reflected. A success toast should confirm the save.
Actual Result: Colors saved. Toast confirmed. Public page shows updated branding colors.

Test Scenario / Title: Super Admin approves a pending hospital
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the Super Admin can approve a hospital registration request.
Preconditions: User is logged in as super_admin. Admin Dashboard (/admin) shows at least 1 pending hospital.
Test Data / Inputs: Hospital to approve: 'New Smile Dental' (status: pending)
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Log in as Super Admin.
2. On the Admin Dashboard, look at the 'Pending Approvals' section.
3. Find 'New Smile Dental' in the pending list.
4. Click the 'Approve' button next to it.
5. Wait for the action to complete.
Expected Result: A success toast should appear: 'New Smile Dental approved'. The hospital should move from the Pending list. The hospital status should change to 'Approved'. The hospital's admin should now be able to log in and access their dashboard.
Actual Result: Toast: 'New Smile Dental approved'. Hospital removed from pending list. Stats updated.

Test Scenario / Title: Super Admin rejects a pending hospital
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the Super Admin can reject a hospital registration and the hospital admin is informed.
Preconditions: Admin Dashboard shows at least 1 pending hospital.
Test Data / Inputs: Hospital to reject: 'Fake Clinic 123' (status: pending)
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. On Admin Dashboard, find 'Fake Clinic 123' in Pending Approvals.
2. Click the 'Reject' button.
3. Wait for the action to complete.
Expected Result: A toast should confirm: 'Fake Clinic 123 rejected'. The hospital should be removed from the pending list. Its status should change to 'rejected'.
Actual Result: Toast: 'Fake Clinic 123 rejected'. Removed from pending. Status changed.

Test Scenario / Title: View expiring subscriptions list
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the Admin Dashboard shows hospitals whose subscriptions are expiring within 30 days.
Preconditions: Super Admin is logged in. At least one hospital has a subscription ending within 30 days.
Test Data / Inputs: No specific input — system data.
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Log in as Super Admin.
2. On the Admin Dashboard, look at the 'Expiring Subscriptions' section.
3. Check if hospitals with subscriptions ending within 30 days are listed.
4. Verify each entry shows hospital name, subscription plan (trial/basic/premium/enterprise), and days remaining.
Expected Result: Hospitals expiring within 30 days should be listed. Each entry should show: hospital name, plan type badge, and 'Expires in X days'. A 'Send Reminder' button should be visible.
Actual Result: Expiring hospitals listed correctly. Plan badges and days remaining visible. Send Reminder button present.

Test Scenario / Title: Patient views their upcoming appointments
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that a logged-in patient can see their upcoming dental appointments on the patient dashboard.
Preconditions: User is logged in as patient. Patient has at least one upcoming appointment.
Test Data / Inputs: Patient login: patient@gmail.com
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Log in as patient.
2. After redirect to Patient Dashboard (/patient), look for the 'Upcoming Appointments' section.
3. Check if your scheduled appointments are listed.
4. Verify each appointment shows: doctor name, date, time, and status.
Expected Result: The patient should see their upcoming appointments with correct doctor name, date/time, and status (Scheduled or Confirmed). Appointments should be sorted by date (nearest first).
Actual Result: Upcoming appointments displayed. Doctor name, date, time, and status all correct.

Test Scenario / Title: Patient views medical history
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that a patient can view their past visits and treatments in the history section.
Preconditions: User is logged in as patient. Patient has past completed visits.
Test Data / Inputs: Patient login: patient@gmail.com
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Log in as patient.
2. On the Patient Dashboard, look for 'Medical History' or 'Past Visits' section.
3. Click on it or navigate to /patient/history.
4. Check if past visits are listed with details.
Expected Result: Past visits should be listed showing: visit date, doctor name, diagnosis, treatment performed, and status (Completed). The list should be sorted by most recent first.
Actual Result: Past visits listed correctly. Dates, doctors, and treatments all visible.

Test Scenario / Title: Search hospitals by city on the landing page
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the landing page allows users to search for dental hospitals by city name.
Preconditions: App landing page (/) is open. No login required.
Test Data / Inputs: Search: Bangalore
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Open the DentalHub landing page (home page).
2. Find the search bar (usually says 'Search hospitals' or 'Find a clinic near you').
3. Type: Bangalore
4. Press Enter or click the Search icon.
5. Check the results below.
Expected Result: Search results should show hospitals in Bangalore. Each result should show hospital name, city, rating, and a way to view more details.
Actual Result: Results showed hospitals in Bangalore. Names, ratings, and cities displayed correctly.

Test Scenario / Title: Dashboard sidebar on mobile screen
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the dashboard sidebar collapses into a hamburger menu on mobile screen sizes.
Preconditions: User is logged in. Dashboard page is open on a mobile device or browser resized to mobile width (375px).
Test Data / Inputs: Device: iPhone 15 (375 × 812) or browser resized to 375px wide.
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Open the Dashboard on a mobile phone or resize the browser to 375px wide.
2. Check if the full sidebar is visible or hidden.
3. If hidden, look for a hamburger menu icon (☰ or three lines) at the top.
4. Tap/click the hamburger icon.
5. Check if sidebar slides out with all navigation links.
Expected Result: On mobile, the sidebar should be hidden by default. A hamburger menu icon should appear. Tapping it should slide the sidebar in from the left. All navigation links (Patients, Doctors, Appointments, etc.) should be accessible.
Actual Result: Sidebar hidden on mobile. Hamburger icon visible. Sidebar slides in when tapped. All links accessible.
Comments / Notes: Responsive sidebar works well.

Test Scenario / Title: Patient list table on small screen
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the patients list table is readable on a small mobile screen without horizontal scrolling issues.
Preconditions: User is logged in. Patients page is open on mobile (375px wide). Multiple patients exist.
Test Data / Inputs: Device: Mobile browser (375px width)
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Open Patients page on a mobile device.
2. Scroll through the patient list.
3. Check if all important columns (Name, Phone, Last Visit) are visible.
4. Check if the table scrolls horizontally if needed or if cards/tiles are used instead.
Expected Result: On mobile, the patient list should either: (a) show a card/tile layout instead of a table, or (b) allow horizontal scrolling. Key info (name, phone) must be readable without zooming.
Actual Result: Patients page shows card layout on mobile instead of table. Name and phone visible. Cards stack vertically. Looks clean.
Comments / Notes: Good mobile-responsive design choice — cards instead of tables.

Test Scenario / Title: Receptionist cannot access Admin Panel
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that a user with the 'receptionist' role cannot access the Super Admin panel by directly typing the URL.
Preconditions: User is logged in as receptionist (not as super_admin).
Test Data / Inputs: Action: Navigate to /admin directly in the browser URL bar.
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Log in with a receptionist account.
2. In the browser address bar, manually type the Admin panel URL (e.g. https://dentizhub.netlify.app/admin).
3. Press Enter.
4. Observe what happens.
Expected Result: The app should either redirect the receptionist back to /dashboard or show an 'Access Denied' / 'Unauthorized' message. The receptionist should NOT see the Super Admin Dashboard.
Actual Result: Receptionist was redirected back to /dashboard. Admin panel was not accessible.
Comments / Notes: Middleware authorization works correctly.

Test Scenario / Title: Patient cannot access hospital dashboard
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that a patient cannot access staff-only pages like /dashboard/patients by URL manipulation.
Preconditions: User is logged in as patient.
Test Data / Inputs: Action: Navigate to /dashboard/patients directly.
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Log in with a patient account.
2. In the browser address bar, type: /dashboard/patients.
3. Press Enter.
4. Observe what happens.
Expected Result: The patient should be blocked from accessing the hospital dashboard. They should be redirected to /patient or shown an 'Access Denied' message.
Actual Result: Patient was redirected to /patient. Could not access staff dashboard.
Comments / Notes: Role-based access control works.

Test Scenario / Title: Dashboard page load time
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the main hospital dashboard loads within 3 seconds on a standard internet connection.
Preconditions: User is logged in as hospital_admin. Hospital has 50+ patients and 100+ past appointments.
Test Data / Inputs: Standard broadband connection (50 Mbps+).
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Log in and wait for the dashboard to load.
2. Use a stopwatch or browser dev tools (Network tab) to measure load time.
3. Measure from clicking 'Dashboard' in sidebar to seeing all stats and recent appointments fully loaded.
Expected Result: The dashboard page should fully render (all stat cards, recent appointments, quick action buttons) within 3 seconds. No visible loading spinners should remain after 3 seconds.
Actual Result: Dashboard loaded in approximately 4.5 seconds. Stats appeared at ~2 seconds, but the recent appointments list took an additional 2.5 seconds to populate.
Bug Description: The hospital dashboard takes approximately 4.5 seconds to fully load — 1.5 seconds longer than the acceptable 3-second threshold. The stat cards load first, but the recent appointments section has a significant delay, likely due to fetching multiple joined records (patient + doctor data for each appointment).
Steps to Reproduce Bug:
1. Log in as hospital_admin.
2. Navigate to Dashboard.
3. Measure total load time until all sections are fully visible.
Actual Result (Bug): Total load time: ~4.5 seconds. Appointments list delays by 2.5 seconds after stats appear.
Expected Result (Bug): Full page should load within 3 seconds, including all sections.
Comments / Notes: Consider pagination for recent appointments, or eager loading with React Query prefetch.

Test Scenario / Title: Add patient with extremely long name (300 characters)
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the system handles an extremely long patient name gracefully.
Preconditions: Patients page is open with Add Patient form.
Test Data / Inputs: Name: AAAAAA... (300 letter A characters) | Phone: 9876543210
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Go to Patients page.
2. Click 'Add Patient'.
3. In the Name field, paste 300 'A' characters.
4. Enter a valid phone number.
5. Click 'Save'.
Expected Result: The app should either: (a) limit the name field to a maximum length (e.g. 255 chars) and prevent typing more, or (b) show a validation error: 'Name is too long. Maximum 255 characters allowed.'
Actual Result: The app accepted all 300 characters and saved the patient. The name is truncated in the patient list card, but hovering shows the full 300-character name. No error or warning was shown.
Bug Description: The patient name field accepts names longer than what the database column can store (likely 255 characters). While it does not crash, the name is silently truncated in some views. No maximum length validation exists on the form.
Steps to Reproduce Bug:
1. Open Add Patient form.
2. Paste 300 characters in the Name field.
3. Save the patient.
4. View the patient in the list — name appears truncated.
Actual Result (Bug): Name accepted without limit. Truncated in list display. No validation warning.
Expected Result (Bug): Name field should either have a maxlength attribute or show a validation warning when exceeding the limit.
Comments / Notes: Low priority but should have basic input length validation.

Test Scenario / Title: Create invoice with negative amount
Test Objective / Purpose: Verify that the billing system does not accept negative values for item prices.
Preconditions: Billing page is open with New Invoice form.
Test Data / Inputs: Patient: Rajesh Kumar | Item: Test Refund, Qty: 1, Unit Price: -5000
Steps to Reproduce / Steps to Execute:
1. Go to Billing page.
2. Click 'New Invoice'.
3. Select Patient: Rajesh Kumar.
4. Add Item: Description: Test Refund, Quantity: 1, Unit Price: -5000.
5. Click 'Create Invoice'.
Expected Result: The app should show a validation error: 'Price cannot be negative.' The invoice should NOT be created with a negative amount.
Actual Result: Invoice was created with total of -₹5,000. Payment status: Pending. This makes no sense in the billing context.
Bug Description: The billing/invoicing system accepts negative values for item unit prices. This allows creating invoices with negative totals (e.g. -₹5,000). There is no validation to prevent this, which could corrupt financial reports and revenue calculations.
Steps to Reproduce Bug:
1. Create new invoice.
2. Add item with unit price: -5000.
3. Save invoice.
4. Invoice shows negative total.
Actual Result (Bug): Invoice created with -₹5,000 total. No validation error.
Expected Result (Bug): System should reject negative prices and show: 'Price must be a positive number.'
Comments / Notes: Could impact financial reports. Add min=0 validation on price input field.
